sentiment.label: risk-on
sentiment.summary: Risk appetite is firm — money is favouring higher-yield currencies over safe havens.

brief.headline: Peace deal cools oil; a hawkish, new-look Fed keeps the dollar firm
brief.summary: Risk appetite improved after the US and Iran signed a framework to end their war, pulling oil prices lower and lifting Asian stocks, though some US Republicans are already questioning whether the deal will hold. The dollar is sitting near a two-month high as new Federal Reserve chair Kevin Warsh held rates but left the door open to a hike and launched a sweeping review of how the central bank runs. Attention now turns to the Bank of England's decision later today, after UK inflation refused to cool. Central banks are pulling in different directions: Brazil and Zimbabwe cut rates while Indonesia and the Philippines look set to hike to defend their currencies against the strong dollar. The yen remains the weak spot near multi-year lows, with Tokyo acknowledging the strain on households but giving no fresh intervention signal, while cheaper oil is helping the Indian rupee firm.

pairsToWatch.note: Near multi-year highs; Japan flags the household cost of a weak yen but signals no imminent intervention.
The Bank of England's decision today is the swing factor for the pound after sticky UK inflation.
Cheaper oil and calmer geopolitics are helping the rupee firm, trimming remittance payouts.
Brazil's third straight rate cut and a hawkish Fed leave the real on the back foot.

calendar: Bank of England interest-rate decision due later today
Bank Indonesia and Philippine central bank rate decisions expected
Markets watching for any Japanese signals on the weak yen
brief.spendIdea: With the yen near multi-year lows, Japan is unusually affordable right now — pricing out a trip or pre-paying tours in yen could stretch a travel budget further.
brief.transferTip: If you're sending money to India, the rupee has firmed on cheaper oil, so you'll get slightly fewer rupees per dollar today than last week — worth watching for pullbacks rather than rushing a large transfer.

categories.label: World News
categories.description: Geopolitics, elections, trade and conflict — the world events that move currencies.

categories.label: Central Banks
categories.description: Rate decisions, interventions, QE and speeches from central banks anywhere in the world.

categories.label: FX Analysis
categories.description: Daily currency commentary and forecasts from banks and research desks.

categories.label: Market Movers
categories.description: Economic data, commodities and risk sentiment with a direct line to FX.

items.headline: US and Iran sign a memorandum of understanding to formally end the war
items.summary: US President Donald Trump and Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian signed a memorandum of understanding to end the recent war, formalised at a post-G7 dinner in France. Tehran's chief negotiator says Iran plans to charge ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz once a 60-day negotiation period ends.
items.whyItMatters: Easing Middle East conflict tends to cool oil prices and trim the safe-haven demand that had been supporting the US dollar.

items.headline: US and Iran sign framework to end war, lifting stocks and cooling oil
items.summary: The United States and Iran signed a framework agreement to end their war, sending Brent crude lower and lifting stock markets in Japan, South Korea and Taiwan. The easing of Middle East tensions improved global risk appetite.
items.whyItMatters: Calmer geopolitics and cheaper oil tend to support risk-sensitive and oil-importing currencies while easing inflation pressure worldwide.

items.headline: Trump warns US will 'go back to shooting' if Iran misbehaves as G7 eyes Lebanon
items.summary: President Trump said the Iran deal is 'not final' and warned of renewed strikes, while G7 leaders called for a wider ceasefire. JD Vance said the text would be released by Friday.
items.whyItMatters: Any sign the fragile Iran truce could break puts a floor under oil and the safe-haven dollar.

items.headline: Strait of Hormuz reopens, but shippers stay cautious on safety
items.summary: The vital oil shipping lane has reopened, though operators and insurers are waiting to see whether passage is genuinely safe before returning.
items.whyItMatters: Hormuz carries a fifth of the world's oil — its reopening is why crude and inflation fears are easing today.

items.headline: G7 agrees new steps to pressure Russia as Trump weighs more sanctions
items.summary: Leaders closed the G7 summit agreeing fresh measures against Moscow, with Trump saying the US is 'looking at' further sanctions.
items.whyItMatters: Tighter Russia sanctions can jolt energy markets and the currencies of countries that trade with Moscow.

items.headline: US-Iran agreement draws backlash from some Republicans
items.summary: Several Republican senators, including Louisiana's Bill Cassidy, sharply criticised the US-Iran memorandum of understanding, calling it a major foreign-policy mistake. The pushback raises questions about how durable the deal will prove.
items.whyItMatters: If doubts grow about whether the deal holds, the recent risk-on mood and oil-price relief that have supported many currencies could quickly reverse.

items.headline: Starmer signals a cabinet job for Andy Burnham amid leadership chatter
items.summary: The PM called the Greater Manchester mayor a 'huge asset' as Labour MPs openly float Burnham as a future leader, should he win an upcoming byelection.
items.whyItMatters: Leadership uncertainty at the top of government is a slow drag on the pound until the picture settles.

items.headline: Fed holds rates steady in Kevin Warsh's debut as chair
items.summary: The Federal Reserve kept rates unchanged at new Chair Kevin Warsh's first meeting, with no surprise in the decision itself — attention is on how he reshapes the Fed's communication.
items.whyItMatters: How Warsh signals the path ahead, more than today's hold, will set the dollar's direction into year-end.

items.headline: UK inflation unexpectedly holds steady ahead of the BoE decision
items.summary: British inflation came in flat rather than cooling further, complicating the Bank of England's rate call due shortly.
items.whyItMatters: Stickier inflation makes BoE cuts harder to justify, a near-term support for the pound.

items.headline: Warsh opens his Fed chairmanship with a sweeping review as rates hold
items.summary: In his first meeting as Federal Reserve chair, Kevin Warsh kept interest rates unchanged and launched a broad review of how the central bank operates. The move signals potential changes to Fed strategy and communication.
items.whyItMatters: A shake-up in how the Fed sets and explains policy could reshape expectations for US rates, a key driver of the dollar's strength.

items.headline: Bank of England set to announce its latest interest-rate decision today
items.summary: The Bank of England delivers its interest-rate decision later today, after UK inflation held steady rather than cooling. Markets are weighing whether policymakers hold or shift their guidance.
items.whyItMatters: Rate decisions are among the biggest single drivers of a currency's value, making today's call pivotal for the British pound.

items.headline: Brazil's central bank cuts its key rate to 14.25% despite a worse inflation outlook
items.summary: Brazil's central bank lowered its benchmark interest rate to 14.25%, its third straight cut, even as its own inflation forecasts deteriorated. Policymakers signalled they may ease further.
items.whyItMatters: Lower interest rates usually reduce the return on holding a currency, which can weigh on the Brazilian real against the dollar.

items.headline: Brazil cuts interest rates again but warns fiscal stimulus could blunt policy
items.summary: Brazil's central bank lowered its benchmark interest rate for a third straight meeting, but cautioned that government fiscal stimulus may weaken the impact of its cuts while inflation stays hot.
items.whyItMatters: Falling Brazilian rates can pressure the real, affecting the cost of goods and travel for anyone dealing in the currency.

items.headline: Indonesia and Philippines move to raise rates as caution prevails
items.summary: Bank Indonesia and the Philippine central bank are both expected to lift interest rates, opting for caution amid a firm US dollar and global uncertainty.
items.whyItMatters: Higher rates can help shield the rupiah and peso from a strong dollar, steadying costs for anyone holding or sending those currencies.

items.headline: ECB's Sleijpen says a repeat of 2022 inflation is unlikely but not ruled out
items.summary: The ECB policymaker struck a calmer tone on price pressures, suggesting the worst of the war-driven inflation scare may be passing.
items.whyItMatters: A less alarmed ECB means less pressure to keep hiking — a mild headwind for the euro.

items.headline: Fed expected to hold as markets parse Warsh's first meeting
items.summary: FXStreet notes the Fed was widely expected to keep rates steady, with the real signal coming from how the new chair frames policy going forward.
items.whyItMatters: The dollar's next leg hinges on Warsh's tone, not the rate itself — expect choppy trading around the press conference.

items.headline: The BoJ hiked to 1.0% — so why is the yen still falling?
items.summary: Analysts say the Bank of Japan's latest hike to 1.0% wasn't enough to trigger a sustained yen recovery, with wide rate gaps to the dollar still dominating.
items.whyItMatters: Even with Japan finally raising rates, the yen stays weak — good for travel to Japan, painful for yen holders.

items.headline: Euro slips below 1.1600 with the Fed in the spotlight
items.summary: EUR/USD gave back recent gains, easing under 1.1600 as traders turned cautious ahead of the Fed decision and Warsh's debut.
items.whyItMatters: 1.1600 is the line in the sand for the euro this week — a Fed surprise decides which way it breaks.

items.headline: Yen sits near its weakest since July 2024 as intervention fears keep bears cautious
items.summary: The dollar held above the mid-160s against the yen, close to its strongest in nearly two years, while the US-Iran deal trimmed some of the dollar's safe-haven support. Traders are wary that Japanese authorities could step in to slow the yen's slide.
items.whyItMatters: A weak yen makes imports and overseas travel pricier for Japanese households and raises the chance authorities intervene to steady it.

items.headline: Pound steadies from a two-month low ahead of the Bank of England decision
items.summary: Sterling recovered from its weakest level since early April as a softer dollar gave it room to bounce, with traders now focused on the Bank of England's interest-rate decision later today.
items.whyItMatters: The Bank of England's decision could set the pound's direction, affecting the cost of UK travel, imports and money transfers.

items.headline: US retail sales jump 0.9% in May, beating expectations
items.summary: American consumers spent more than expected in May, with sales up 0.9% versus the 0.5% forecast and broad strength across categories.
items.whyItMatters: A strong consumer gives the Fed less reason to cut, which tends to support the dollar.

items.headline: Japan flags weak-yen burden on households but signals no imminent action
items.summary: Chief Cabinet Secretary Minoru Kihara acknowledged that a weak yen raises costs for Japanese households, a slightly more dovish framing, but gave no fresh signal that the government is preparing to intervene.
items.whyItMatters: Without a clear intervention warning, the yen could keep weakening, which lowers the cost of Japanese goods and travel for foreign-currency holders.

items.headline: New Zealand dollar weakens as confidence hits a two-year low
items.summary: NZD/USD slipped toward 0.5820 as investors cut risk ahead of the Fed and local confidence fell to its lowest since 2023.
items.whyItMatters: The kiwi is a classic risk gauge — its slide shows investors playing defence into the Fed.

items.headline: Oil prices fall sharply as Iran supply outlook improves
items.summary: Crude dropped, holding below $80, as the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz and an improving Iran supply picture eased fears.
items.whyItMatters: Falling oil cools the inflation that drove this year's rate hikes — the day's biggest cross-currency theme.

items.headline: Dollar holds near two-month high as Fed rate-hike bets grow and yen slides
items.summary: The US dollar held close to a two-month high as traders raised bets that the Federal Reserve could still hike rates this year. The Japanese yen weakened further, sliding toward multi-year lows.
items.whyItMatters: A stronger dollar makes travel, imports and dollar debt more expensive for those earning in other currencies, while the weak yen makes Japan cheaper to visit.

items.headline: Egypt's pound becomes the world's top currency as the oil shock reverses
items.summary: The Egyptian pound has rallied to lead global currencies as falling energy prices unwind the pressure that had weighed on it.
items.whyItMatters: A surging pound changes the math on transfers to Egypt — fewer pounds per dollar sent home.

items.headline: Gold slides toward $4,250 after the Fed holds rates but flags a possible hike
items.summary: Gold fell to around $4,280 an ounce after the US Federal Reserve kept rates on hold but signalled borrowing costs could still rise later this year. The hawkish tone lifted the dollar and pressured the metal.
items.whyItMatters: A firmer dollar and the prospect of higher US rates make non-yielding gold less attractive and point to a stronger greenback against other currencies.

items.headline: Rupee firms as peace deal cools oil, trimming remittance payouts
items.summary: The Indian rupee rebounded as the US-Iran peace deal pushed oil prices lower, easing one of India's biggest import costs. The stronger rupee slightly reduces the payout on inbound remittances.
items.whyItMatters: A firmer rupee means people sending money to India get fewer rupees per dollar, so timing transfers around these swings can matter.

items.headline: Eurozone core inflation tops forecasts, casting doubt on further ECB rate cuts
items.summary: Core inflation in the eurozone came in higher than expected, raising questions about how much more the European Central Bank can cut rates. The data lent some support to the euro's outlook.
items.whyItMatters: Stickier inflation makes rate cuts less likely, which tends to support a currency by keeping its yield higher for longer.

items.headline: Sterling looks past cooler inflation with the BoE in focus
items.summary: The pound shrugged off softer inflation details as traders set up for the Bank of England's decision.
items.whyItMatters: Sterling is in wait-and-see mode — the BoE, not the data, is what moves it next.

items.headline: Indian rupee jumps to 94.29 as cheaper oil and easing tensions lift sentiment
items.summary: The Indian rupee strengthened by 31 paise to 94.29 per dollar as falling oil prices and the easing of US-Iran tensions improved sentiment. India imports most of its oil, so cheaper crude eases pressure on the currency.
items.whyItMatters: For an oil-importing economy like India, lower crude prices cut the dollars needed to pay for energy, supporting the rupee.

currencyViews.summary: The dollar is holding near a two-month high as traders lean toward the Fed staying higher for longer. The US–Iran peace framework trims some of its safe-haven support, but the rate story is winning out.

The yen sits near its weakest since July 2024. Even the Bank of Japan's hike to 1.0% hasn't stemmed the slide while the gap to US rates stays wide — and the market is watching for possible intervention.

The euro slipped below 1.1600 with the Fed in focus, and the signals conflict: the ECB's Sleijpen played down inflation risks, yet core inflation came in hotter than expected. Direction likely waits on the Fed.
The rupee firmed toward 94.29 as the US–Iran deal pushed oil lower. Cheaper crude eases India's biggest import cost, taking pressure off the currency.

The real is under pressure after the central bank cut its key rate a third straight time to 14.25% while warning that fiscal stimulus could blunt policy. A hawkish Fed adds emerging-market outflow risk.
Sterling steadied off a two-month low ahead of the Bank of England. UK inflation held steady rather than cooling, keeping the rate decision genuinely live — so the pound is waiting on the BoE.
